APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper addresses event linking/event coreference, a central NLP problem, using modern neural techniques (CNN-based sentential representations and a trainable similarity network) and thus falls squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope. It targets the right audience by comparing to relevant prior work (Chen & Ji, Cybulska & Vossen, Lee et al., etc.), reports state-of-the-art results on ACE 2005 and a larger ACE++ corpus, and includes error analysis and hyperparameter reporting—practices expected at these venues. The use of standard datasets (and a larger augmented set), neural modelling, and empirical comparison make the problem formulation and evaluation aligned with top-tier expectations.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and presents its approach in a logical sequence (methodology describing two CNNs, similarity network, and training with Adam) and states its main contribution—an event linking model using sentential CNN features—early. Notation and experimental details appear sufficiently reported (e.g., hyperparameters are given, related work coverage is adequate), and the writing quality is described as good. A minor presentation gap is the omission of stated evaluation metrics in the summary, which makes the experimental reporting slightly less clear.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies convolutional neural nets to produce latent sentential features and a trainable single-layer similarity network (with a square activation) for within-document event linking, avoiding external knowledge bases—an approach that is a non-trivial engineering extension of prior coreference methods and yields state-of-the-art results on ACE and a larger ACE++ set. However, event linking/coreference is a well-studied problem and CNN-based sentence representations and learned similarity functions are established techniques, so the contribution is more an incremental/empirical advance than a fundamentally new idea. The authors clearly differentiate their design from prior work (no KB, two CNNs, novel similarity layer), but novelty is moderate rather than high.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper presents empirical results claiming state-of-the-art performance on ACE 2005 and a larger ACE++ corpus, which supports the main claim, but the summary does not specify the evaluation metrics used. There is no mention of ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the CNN or similarity components, limiting understanding of what drives gains. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the paper includes an error analysis and stated limitations, which is positive. Reproducibility is only partially supported: hyperparameters are reported, but code availability and details about the ACE++ construction or exact evaluation procedures are not mentioned.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (CNN-based sentential features, a trainable square-activation similarity layer, and logistic scoring) and reports state-of-the-art results against reasonable baselines on ACE 2005 and a larger ACE++ corpus, with hyperparameters and an error analysis provided. However, important experimental details are missing from the summary (evaluation metrics not specified, no mention of statistical significance tests or data splits), and ACE++ is an author-created corpus which risks bias and reduces reproducibility unless fully described. Mathematical content appears straightforward (no complex proofs) and the training procedure is standard, so there are no obvious derivation errors, but the empirical support is incomplete as presented. Overall the work is promising but some methodological/experimental evidence gaps prevent a higher technical score.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important task of within-document event linking (coreference) and removes reliance on external knowledge bases by using CNN-derived sentential features, which is a practically valuable shift. It reports state-of-the-art performance on both ACE 2005 and a larger ACE++ dataset created by the authors, which suggests the approach can change how researchers prioritize sentential representation over KB-dependent features. By introducing a trainable similarity function and providing error analysis, it opens clear directions for follow-up work and is likely to be adopted or built upon in information extraction, though it is not clearly transformative across NLP as a whole.

REPLICABILITY.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters and gives a clear high-level model description (CNNs, single-layer similarity network, Adam training), so core model/training details appear present. However, the data preprocessing pipeline is not described, evaluation metrics and procedures are not specified, and the ACE++ corpus (authors’ enlarged dataset) is not clearly available; code is not mentioned. Given these omissions, the method is only partially reproducible from the provided description.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against several relevant prior systems (Chen and Ji 2009; Cybulska and Vossen 2015; Lee et al. 2012; Sachan et al. 2015; Araki and Mitamura 2015) and reports state-of-the-art results on ACE 2005 and the larger ACE++ set. It clearly explains how its CNN-based sentential representations and trainable similarity differ from prior approaches and indicates adequate related-work coverage. However, the summary does not specify evaluation metrics or confirm identical evaluation conditions, and it is not stated whether prior methods sometimes perform comparably or better, so fairness/complete acknowledgement are unclear.

main_claims: Proposes an event linking (within-document event coreference) model that does not rely on external knowledge bases and instead uses sentential features from convolutional neural networks.

Uses two convolutional neural networks to generate latent sentential representations for event-mention candidates and their contexts, which are augmented with lexical and pairwise features for linking decisions.
Introduces a trainable similarity function (single-layer NN with square activation) over the concatenated representations and outputs a coreference score via logistic regression.
Reports state-of-the-art performance on two datasets (ACE 2005 and a larger ACE++ dataset created by the authors), and provides an error analysis highlighting future research directions.
_summary.methodology: Two CNNs produce latent sentential feature vectors for each event mention and its context; these vectors are concatenated with lexical and pairwise features, passed through a single-layer similarity network using a square activation to produce a distributed similarity vector, and finally scored by a logistic regression classifier; the model is trained with Adam minimizing logistic loss.
limitations: The ACE 2005 corpus is small (599 documents), motivating the creation of the larger ACE++ dataset.
The paper reports an error analysis and indicates directions for further research (i.e., remaining failures are acknowledged but not fully resolved).
